authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1, Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-34 Rev. 1 directly defines contingency planning workflow, BIA, recovery objectives, alternate processing, backup, testing, training, and maintenance for information systems.
practical_use_in_pack: BIA, RTO, RPO, and dependency evidence

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-160 Volume 2 Revision 1, Developing Cyber-Resilient Systems
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 Rev. 1 is a recognized cyber resiliency engineering reference for objectives, techniques, design principles, adversarial disruption, and tradeoff analysis.

authoritative_sources.title: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is official cross-industry cybersecurity risk-management guidance that defines Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover outcomes used to frame cyber recovery and resilience decisions.

authoritative_sources.title: StopRansomware Guide
authoritative_sources.publisher: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), Multi-State Information Sharing and Analysis Center (MS-ISAC), National Security Agency (NSA), and Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA and partner agencies publish operational ransomware guidance for prevention, backup, response coordination, and recovery, which is directly authoritative for ransomware-like destructive recovery planning.

authoritative_sources.title: Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA's cross-sector goals provide prioritized cybersecurity practices for critical services and organizations of varied maturity, making them authoritative for cross-industry resilience baseline expectations.

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5, Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is the recognized federal control catalog with contingency planning, incident response, audit, and integrity controls that define control evidence expectations.

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8, Control 11 Data Recovery
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are widely used consensus safeguards; Control 11 directly addresses data recovery processes, automated backups, backup protection, and restore testing evidence.

authoritative_sources.title: Business Continuity Management Booklet
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FFIEC examination guidance is authoritative for financial institution business continuity management, resilience, recovery, third-party, testing, and governance expectations, providing concrete sector operating constraints for cyber recovery.

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC final rules establish disclosure obligations for public companies' material cybersecurity incidents and governance, constraining executive communications and legal disclosure boundaries during recovery.

authoritative_sources.title: Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act of 2022 (CIRCIA)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the CIRCIA rulemaking and guidance authority; this source constrains critical-infrastructure incident and ransom payment reporting claims and therefore recovery communications and escalation boundaries.

workflow_stages.name: request intake and authority classification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm requested output, requested authority, affected services, tenant context, recovery stage, execution requests, legal or audit claims, adjacent lanes, and hard refusal triggers before analysis.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: critical service and BIA evidence assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect asset inventory, service criticality, BIA, maximum tolerable downtime, RTO/RPO, data classification, service owner, continuity policy, and outage scenario evidence from systems of record.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: dependency and recovery environment validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate identity, DNS, network, cloud, storage, database, endpoint, third-party, monitoring, communications, backup vault, DRaaS, clean-room, and restore environment dependencies.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: backup integrity and restore readiness assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess backup scope, job history, immutable or offline copy status, restore tests, malware or integrity scans, retention constraints, and fallback restore points before recovery sequencing.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: recovery objective and strategy design
workflow_stages.description: Translate impact, RTO/RPO, dependency, incident state, and backup evidence into prioritized recovery strategy, service tiers, prerequisites, validation gates, rollback triggers, and owner routing.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: test, evidence, and executive checkpoint planning
workflow_stages.description: Define recovery exercise scope, pass/fail criteria, evidence capture, communications checks, decision checkpoints, signoff routing, and readiness artifacts without issuing attestation.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: escalation, refusal, and handoff
workflow_stages.description: Return to orchestrator or route to adjacent owners when evidence is missing or conflicting, authority shifts, privileged execution is requested, or regulated, safety, legal, disclosure, audit, or architecture decisions determine the outcome.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: asset inventory, CMDB records, service catalog, owner map, data classification, and criticality register
business impact analysis, maximum tolerable downtime, RTO/RPO targets, recovery tiers, outage scenarios, and continuity policy
service dependency map covering identity, DNS, network, cloud, storage, database, endpoint, third-party, monitoring, communications, and administrative trust dependencies
backup architecture, backup job logs, immutable or offline backup evidence, restore test reports, integrity or malware scan results, clean-room readiness, and DRaaS or replication status
incident or outage timeline, affected-system list, containment state, data-loss indicators, service health dashboard, and monitoring or SIEM evidence
tenant recovery runbook, DR plan, crisis communication plan, escalation tree, approval matrix, legal/regulatory routing, evidence retention policy, and exception register
resilience recovery plan, restoration roadmap, rollback and escalation guidance, acceptance criteria checklist, executive decision memo, evidence gap checklist, and out-of-scope handoff note
systems_of_record: asset inventory, CMDB, service catalog, and ownership registry
business continuity or BIA platform, GRC repository, risk register, control evidence repository, and exception register
backup platform, immutable backup vault, DRaaS or replication console, restore test repository, clean-room recovery records, and storage administration records
dependency mapping repository, architecture repository, IaC repository, cloud account inventory, network source of truth, DNS records, and identity platform records
incident ticketing, outage management, SIEM or security event records, monitoring platform, service health dashboard, and post-incident action tracker
tenant policy repository, continuity plan library, communication archive, legal/regulatory routing inventory, cyber insurance record, contract repository, and approval matrix

allowed: Recommend evidence-backed recovery priorities, RTO/RPO assumptions, dependency sequencing, validation checks, rollback triggers, and escalation points.
Draft advisory contingency and recovery planning artifacts without executing production changes or tenant-specific runbook updates.
Design recovery tests, evidence checklists, executive decision memos, and posture improvement roadmaps using named sources and tenant evidence.
Identify missing records, source conflicts, domain failure modes, and adjacent specialist handoffs.
escalate: BIA, criticality, dependency, backup, restore, incident-impact, service owner, approval, tenant policy, or source authority evidence is missing or contradictory.
Sector, safety, privacy, financial, contractual, cyber insurance, public-company, critical-infrastructure, or regulatory facts could change recovery timing, communication, or release.
The request requires privileged vendor tooling, production failover, restore execution, backup deletion, retention override, or customer-specific policy or runbook change.
Public guidance and tenant policy conflict without a named source precedence decision.
refuse: Live incident response containment, eradication, host isolation, account disablement, firewall changes, restore execution, failover execution, or privileged operations.
Penetration testing, exploit validation, adversary emulation, vulnerability proof of concept, malware analysis, forensic acquisition, or chain-of-custody decisions.
Formal audit attestation, legal compliance certification, breach or materiality determination, regulator filing, public disclosure wording, or safety-release signoff.
Broad enterprise architecture ownership, procurement, budget approval, risk acceptance, or tenant-specific policy and operational playbook changes.

domain_failure_modes: Generic continuity advice ignores cyber-specific backup compromise, administrative trust loss, malware persistence, data-integrity uncertainty, and clean-room recovery needs.
Recovery order is guessed from service names instead of BIA, RTO/RPO, dependency, backup, incident-impact, and owner evidence.
Backup success is treated as recovery readiness without restore tests, integrity validation, immutability or offline copy evidence, retention constraints, and malware scan context.

The specialist drifts into incident response execution, security operations, digital forensics, penetration testing, malware analysis, broad architecture, audit, legal, or crisis communications ownership.
Regulated or safety-sensitive tenants receive recovery timing or communication recommendations without sector authority, safety facts, approval evidence, or legal/regulatory review.
Public framework mapping is overstated as compliance certification, audit opinion, cyber insurance determination, or production release authority.
Tenant-specific policy, runbook, or vendor-console instructions are produced without owner approval, retrieval controls, or rollback authorization.
Conflicting public source, tenant policy, contract, regulator, or insurer guidance is hidden instead of recorded and escalated.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: sector-specific recovery timing and reporting obligations
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Public-company, critical-infrastructure, healthcare, financial services, insurance, contract, privacy, safety, and state breach obligations vary by tenant and jurisdiction. The pack routes final determinations to legal/regulatory, safety, or authorized human reviewers.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: backup integrity and malware-free release certainty
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The specialist can request and assess evidence but cannot certify a restore point as clean or release a system; final release requires authorized owner validation and, where needed, incident response or forensics review.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: architecture redesign versus cyber resilience recommendation
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The specialist may identify resilience-relevant architecture gaps and verification checkpoints but must route broad security, cloud, network, identity, or enterprise architecture ownership to adjacent specialists.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated spec-pack use because disputed sector, safety, legal, audit, release, and architecture authority is explicitly routed rather than guessed.

Research constrains the scenario suite and manifest by requiring named cyber recovery records, systems of record, source freshness, evidence traceability, and refusal of execution or attestation authority.
